title: Degree audit: mapping a transcript to a diploma
summary: A degree audit answers the question every student asks: 'what's left before I graduate?' Today: how an audit maps completed and in-progress courses to requirement blocks, what a 'what-if' audit does, and why a clean audit still doesn't guarantee a diploma.

activities.title: What a degree audit does
activities.markdown:
A **degree audit** is an advising document that **maps a student's degree requirements against their academic transcript**, giving a real-time read on progress toward graduation.

It evaluates **completed, in-progress, and planned** coursework — in-progress courses typically show an **'IP'** grade. The requirements are organized into **requirement blocks** (degree / major / minor) made of rules: credit counts, GPA minimums, specific courses, and electives.

A powerful feature is the **'What-If' audit**: it hypothetically applies a **different major or minor** to show how existing courses would fit and what would remain — **without changing** the student's actual program.

activities.question: What does a degree audit do?
options: Maps a student's completed/in-progress courses against program requirements
Calculates the student's financial aid award
activities.explanation: A degree audit compares the transcript (completed, in-progress, transfer) against the requirement blocks for the program, showing what's satisfied and what remains.

activities.question: A 'What-If' audit…
options: hypothetically applies a different major/minor without changing the actual one
activities.explanation: A What-If audit is a non-destructive simulation: it shows how existing courses map to a different program so a student can compare paths, without altering their real major.

activities.question: Every requirement box is checked in the audit. Is graduation guaranteed?
options: No — the registrar runs a final audit after final grades post
Only if the GPA is exactly 4.0
activities.explanation: A self-service audit is a planning tool. After a student applies to graduate, the registrar performs a final audit once final grades are in to officially clear the degree.

activities.title: How a degree audit evaluates
activities.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Load requirement blocks] --> B[Pull transcript: done, in-progress, transfer]
  B --> C[Map courses to requirements]
  C --> D[Check unmet rules: GPA, credits]
  D --> E[Show remaining requirements]
  E --> F[Registrar final audit gates graduation]

steps.title: Load requirement blocks
steps.caption: Degree, major, and minor rule sets for the student's catalog year.

steps.title: Map courses → rules
steps.caption: Apply each course to the requirement it satisfies.

steps.title: Check unmet rules
steps.caption: Evaluate remaining credits, GPA minimums, and specific-course rules.

steps.title: Show what's left
steps.caption: Display outstanding requirements to the student/advisor.

steps.title: Final audit
steps.caption: After grades post, the registrar's final audit clears graduation.

activities.caption: The audit engine is a rules engine over a transcript — and a clean self-service audit is necessary but not sufficient.

activities.front: Why is a 'all boxes checked' audit **not** a guaranteed diploma?
activities.back: Because the self-service audit reflects in-progress work and planning. The **registrar's final audit after final grades** is the official graduation clearance — in-progress courses must actually be passed.

items: A **degree audit** = a rules engine mapping the **transcript** to **requirement blocks**.
It counts **completed, in-progress (IP), and transfer** work.
**Graduation clearance** is a separate **final audit** by the registrar after grades post.

glossary.definition: A progress report mapping a student's transcript against degree/major/minor requirements in real time.
A non-destructive simulation applying a hypothetical major/minor to existing courses.
A grouped set of degree/major/minor rules (credits, GPA, specific courses) the audit evaluates.
The registrar's final audit after final grades confirming all requirements are met.

reflection: How would you represent requirement blocks and rule evaluation in ManageArk so a 'what-if' audit reuses the same engine as the real audit — without mutating the student's actual program record?
